senses.definition: a larval stage of some starfish that occurs after bipinnaria and features three forward-facing processes
examples: The scientist described the brachiolarian stage as an intermediate form in starfish development.
Researchers observe brachiolarian larvae under the microscope to study early echinoderm development.

senses.definition: an aquatic invertebrate with a shell that has two hinged valves, living in marine environments
examples: Brachiopods are shelled marine animals that resemble clams but belong to a different group.
Fossils of brachiopods are common in Paleozoic rock layers.

senses.definition: a phylum of marine invertebrates characterized by two shell valves and a lophophore feeding structure
examples: Brachiopoda is an old phylum with many fossil examples, though living species are rare today.
Scientists study Brachiopoda to understand ancient marine ecosystems.

senses.definition: alternative spelling or form related to brachiopod (usage varies by language or historical texts)
examples: The term brachiopode appears in some European taxonomic writings.
Old glossaries may list brachiopode as a synonym in French or other languages.

senses.definition: a person who studies or collects brachiopods
examples: The brachiopodist explained the differences between brachiopod fossils.
A collector and brachiopodist shared rare specimens at the museum.

senses.definition: having brachiopod-like features or a brachiopod-shaped shell
examples: The brachiopodous shell suggested an ancient marine organism.
In fossil illustrations, the brachiopodous form is easy to recognize.

senses.definition: plural of brachiopod; marine invertebrates with two shells
examples: Brachiopods were abundant in ancient seas.
Fossils of brachiopods help scientists study Earth's history.

senses.definition: relating to or near the radius bone in the forearm
examples: The brachioradial muscle attaches near the forearm.
Anatomists describe brachioradial procedures for arm injuries.

senses.definition: a muscle of the forearm that flexes the elbow
examples: The brachioradialis helps bend the elbow when the forearm is in a neutral position.
In anatomy class, we studied the brachioradialis and its function.

senses.definition: relating to the front or anterior of the spinal column (rare technical term)
examples: The brachiorrhachidian nerve path was described in ancient anatomical texts.
In historic anatomy diagrams, brachiorrhachidian terms appear infrequently.

senses.definition: rare term historically referring to a braid-like or threadlike structure in anatomy; not in common modern use
examples: Older texts mention brachiorrheuma in descriptions of fascia.
Brachiorrheuma appears only in specialized dictionaries today.

senses.definition: a very large, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur from the Jurassic period
examples: Brachiosaurs stood among the tallest dinosaurs.
The long neck of a brachiosaur helped it reach high vegetation.

senses.definition: plural form of brachiosaur; very large, long-necked dinosaurs
examples: Brachiosaurs were among the largest land animals.
Fossil bones of brachiosaurs have been found in several continents.

senses.definition: the genus name for a group of large long-necked dinosaurs; commonly used as singular for individuals
examples: Brachiosaurus is famous for its tall neck and lofty stance.
Paleontologists described a brachiosaurus fossil discovered nearby.

senses.definition: plural of brachiosaurus; multiple individuals of the brachiosaurus genus
examples: Several brachiosauruses were unearthed from the same site.
The exhibit featured models of brachiosauruses in different poses.

senses.definition: alt spelling variant for brachiosaurus plural
examples: Museum signs sometimes use brachiosauruses instead of brachiosaurus for clarity.
Scholarly papers occasionally list brachiosauruses in their references.

senses.definition: a rare anatomical term referring to a breach or opening in a bone or structure (historical/rare usage)
examples: Historical texts mention brachiostrophosis in the context of skeletal abnormalities.
The term is not common in modern anatomy.

senses.definition: a surgical incision into the arm or for the arm region
examples: The surgeon performed a brachiotomy to access the damaged vessels.
Postoperative care followed a successful brachiotomy.

senses.definition: a rare term used in anatomy or zoology, often within historical texts referring to a group related to the brachiopods or early chordates (highly specialized)
examples: In old scholarly works, brachistocephali appear as a niche classification.
Modern texts rarely mention brachistocephali.

senses.definition: describing a structure with a head that is linked to a long neck or an elongated head in certain anatomical contexts
examples: The brachistocephalic skull offers insights into evolutionary anatomy.
In the antique diagrams, brachistocephalic features are noted.

senses.definition: describing organisms or structures with a head connected to the body by a long neck
examples: Brachistocephalous forms are discussed in older zoological literature.
The illustration labeled the specimen as brachistocephalous.

senses.definition: a condition characterized by a long neck relative to the head (rare/archaic usage in anatomy)
examples: Some historical case studies mention brachistocephaly.
Modern medicine rarely uses this term.

senses.definition: a curve along which a bead slides under gravity in the shortest time between two points
examples: The brachistochrone problem was a famous puzzle in calculus and physics.
Engineers use the brachistochrone curve in optimal timing problems.

senses.definition: relating to the brachistochrone or its properties
examples: The brachistochronic analysis compared different curves for descent time.
A brachistochronic principle can appear in physics problems.

senses.definition: relating to or resembling the brachistochrone curve
examples: A brachistochronous path minimizes descent time for a bead.
In some texts, brachistochronous motion is discussed alongside other curves.

senses.definition: alternate spelling variant of brachistochronous
examples: Some older manuscripts use brachistochronous or brachistochronous terms interchangeably.
In modern texts, brachistochronous is more common, but brachistochronous appears too.

senses.definition: same as brachistochronous; relating to the brachistochrone curve
examples: The brachistochronous path is the fastest descent under gravity.
Students studied brachistochronous problems in class.

senses.definition: the arm or a limb; a part of an animal's body
examples: The brachium of the monkey swung from branch to branch.
In anatomy, the upper limb is sometimes called the brachium.
